messages.content: shorten：Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease characterized by the excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix components, presenting a significant challenge in the field of medicine. Despite extensive research efforts, effective therapeutic strategies targeting liver fibrosis remain limited. Thus, it is essential to explore innovative approaches for the management of this condition.

Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease with excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix components, posing a major challenge in medicine. Effective treatments are limited, making it crucial to explore innovative approaches for its management.

Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease with excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix components, posing a significant challenge in medicine. Despite extensive research efforts, effective treatments remain limited, highlighting the need for innovative approaches to manage this condition.

Liver fibrosis is a challenging chronic disease characterized by excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix components. Limited effective treatments highlight the need for innovative management approaches.

shorten： C57BL/6J male mice were used to model liver fibrosis, induced by Carbon tetrachloride (CCl4). The fibrotic mice were treated with interleukin-10 (IL-10) gene and cleared NK cells with an Asialo GM1 (ASGM1) antibody. The mice were divided into five groups: normal controls (NORMAL group), liver fibrosis group (FIBROSIS group), IL-10 gene treatment group (IL-10 group), NK cells clearance group (FIBROSIS/ASGM1 group) and IL-10 gene treatment combined with NK cell clearance group (IL-10/ASGM1 group).

C57BL/6J male mice were used to model liver fibrosis induced by CCl4. Fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and cleared NK cells using an ASGM1 antibody. The mice were divided into five groups: NORMAL group (normal controls), FIBROSIS group (liver fibrosis), IL-10 group (IL-10 gene treatment), FIBROSIS/ASGM1 group (NK cells clearance), and IL-10/ASGM1 group (IL-10 gene treatment combined with NK cell clearance).

shorten：C57BL/6J male mice were used to model liver fibrosis induced by CCl4. Fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and cleared NK cells using an ASGM1 antibody. The mice were divided into five groups: NORMAL group (normal controls), FIBROSIS group (liver fibrosis), IL-10 group (IL-10 gene treatment), FIBROSIS/ASGM1 group (NK cells clearance), and IL-10/ASGM1 group (IL-10 gene treatment combined with NK cell clearance).

C57BL/6J male mice were used to model CCl4-induced liver fibrosis. They were divided into five groups: NORMAL, FIBROSIS, IL-10, FIBROSIS/ASGM1, and IL-10/ASGM1. The fibrotic mice in these groups were treated with IL-10 gene and NK cell clearance using an ASGM1 antibody.
shorten：C57BL/6J mice were used to model CCl4-induced liver fibrosis. They were divided into five groups: NORMAL, FIBROSIS, IL-10, FIBROSIS/ASGM1, and IL-10/ASGM1.
C57BL/6J mice were used to model CCl4-induced liver fibrosis and divided into five groups: NORMAL, FIBROSIS, IL-10, FIBROSIS/ASGM1, and IL-10/ASGM1.
shorten：To observe the effect of the presence or absence of NK cells on the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10，fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and cleared NK cells using ASGM1 antibody.
To assess the impact of NK cells on the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10, fibrotic mice were given IL-10 gene treatment and NK cells were cleared using the ASGM1 antibody.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, promoting the NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene effectively reduces liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice. Furthermore, the IL-10 gene enhances the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, leading to increased expression of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a, and promoting NK cell development. It also regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. Conversely, depletion of NK cells from the liver substantially inhibits the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and promotes NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

The presence of NK cells enhances the anti-fibrotic effects of the IL-10 gene, leading to improved liver fibrosis and reduced liver damage in mice. The IL-10 gene stimulates the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, resulting in increased expression of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a, as well as promoting NK cell development. Additionally, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and elevates the level of CCL5. Notably, depletion of NK cells from the liver considerably impairs the IL-10 gene's anti-fibrotic efficacy.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and promotes NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

The presence of NK cells enhances the effectiveness of the IL-10 gene in reducing liver damage and improving liver fibrosis in mice. The IL-10 gene not only stimulates the accumulation of NK cells in the liver but also upregulates the expression of NKG2D and IFN-γ, promoting the development of NK cells and increasing CD107a expression. It also regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases CCL5 levels. However, depleting NK cells from the liver significantly impairs the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10.

improve:In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention significantly alleviated liver injury and collagen fiber deposition, improving mouse liver fibrosis. In addition, it promoted the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and enhanced their function, as evidenced by an increase in activation-related receptor NKG2D and toxic factors IFN-γ and CD107a. At the same time, it also regulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver accompanied by an increase in CCL5 content in the liver. However, when NK cells were exhausted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited.

In the presence of NK cells, intervention with the IL-10 gene effectively reduced liver injury and collagen fiber deposition, thereby improving liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention also promoted the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and enhanced their function, as indicated by increased expression of the activation-related receptor NKG2D and the toxic factors IFN-γ and CD107a. Moreover, it regulated the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver, which was accompanied by an elevation in CCL5 levels. However, depleting NK cells from the liver significantly hindered the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10.

Write a conclusion for these results:In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention effectively reduced liver injury and collagen deposition, improving liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention enhanced the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and boosted their function, as evidenced by increased expression of activation-related receptor NKG2D, as well as the production of inflammatory factors IFN-γ and cytotoxic factor CD107a. Furthermore, IL-10 gene intervention regulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues, accompanied by an elevation in CCL5 levels. However, when NK cells were depleted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited.

In conclusion, our findings demonstrate that IL-10 gene intervention in the presence of NK cells effectively mitigated liver injury and reduced collagen deposition, resulting in improved liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention not only enhanced the accumulation of NK cells in the liver but also augmented their functional capabilities, including the upregulation of activation-related receptor NKG2D and the secretion of inflammatory factor IFN-γ and cytotoxic factor CD107a. Additionally, IL-10 gene intervention modulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues, accompanied by an increase in CCL5 levels. Nonetheless, the depletion of NK cells from the liver significantly hindered the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10. These findings highlight the importance of NK cells and their interaction with IL-10 in the treatment of liver fibrosis, and provide insights for further research and therapeutic development in this area.

improve：The anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 is dependent on enhancing the function of natural killer cells in mouse fibrotic liver, via promoting NK cells activation, cytotoxicity, development and migration, highlighting the potential of targeting NK cells in IL-10 gene intervention as a therapeutic approach for the management of liver fibrosis.

In conclusion, our study highlights the crucial role of natural killer (NK) cells in mediating the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 in mouse fibrotic liver. By promoting NK cell activation, cytotoxicity, development, and migration, IL-10 enhances the overall function of these immune cells. These findings emphasize the potential of targeting NK cells as part of IL-10 gene intervention for the effective management of liver fibrosis. Further exploration of this therapeutic approach holds promise for the development of novel treatments in the field of liver fibrosis research.

improve：The anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 is dependent on enhancing the function of natural killer cells in mouse fibrotic liver, by promoting NK cells activation, cytotoxicity, development and migration, elucidating the mechanisms of IL-10 regulating NK cells to mitigate liver fibrosis progression.

In conclusion, our findings elucidate the underlying mechanisms by which IL-10 exerts its anti-fibrotic effect in mouse fibrotic liver. It relies on enhancing the function of natural killer (NK) cells through promoting their activation, cytotoxicity, development, and migration. These insights into the regulation of NK cells by IL-10 provide valuable knowledge for understanding the progression of liver fibrosis and offer potential therapeutic strategies for its mitigation.

shorten:Liver damage was assessed by hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining and plasma alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) levels. Liver fibrosis was visualized by Sirius Red staining and hydroxyproline assay. The distribution of NK cells in liver tissue was analyzed through immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence. The evaluation of NK cells isolated from liver tissue or blood, such as quantity, activation, cytotoxicity, maturity degrees, and sources, was performed using flow cytometry. The chemotactic factors in fibrotic liver were determined by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA).

Liver damage was assessed using H&E staining and plasma ALT and AST levels. Liver fibrosis was visualized with Sirius Red staining and hydroxyproline assay. NK cell distribution in liver tissue was analyzed through immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence. Flow cytometry was used to evaluate NK cells isolated from liver tissue or blood, including their quantity, activation, cytotoxicity, maturity degrees, and sources. Chemotactic factors in fibrotic liver were determined using ELISA.
improve：However, when NK cells were depleted from the liver, carbon tetrachloride induced liver fibrosis injury was more severe, and the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited
Depletion of NK cells from the liver exacerbated carbon tetrachloride-induced liver fibrosis injury and significantly impaired the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10.
